text: This is Dr. Rani Abaza. This is a robotic prostatectomy with lymph node dissection. We do it as a transperitoneal operation with a total of five incisions. So we've got the cautery scissor in the right hand, fenestrated Maryland bipolar in the left hand, and then the prograsp in the fourth arm. We have the patient in Trendelenburg, and we have the camera port at the umbilicus. We have one single eight millimeter air seal assistant port, and that eight millimeter port is what gives us our air seal insufflation, and the assistant will use that to hand me needles and to put the bag for the specimen and things like that. They won't be using that for suction because I'll be doing my own suctioning.

The surgeon releases the bowel and performs a posterior dissection to expose the seminal vesicles. An initial incision is made above the peritoneal reflection on the bladder. The fourth robotic arm is used to visualize the lateral aspect of the seminal vesicles by positioning it towards the contralateral shoulder. Monopolar and bipolar cautery are used at a setting of twenty-five to minimize thermal spread. The vas deferens is exposed and lifted with the fourth arm for better visualization, ensuring the seminal vesicles are free of surrounding tissue.

The surgeon cuts the vas deferens in line with the lateral aspect of the seminal vesicle, allowing both ends to retract. An incision is made to the right of the ligament, extending down to the pubic bone. The surgeon performs blunt dissection, cleaning fat from the prostate, and uses cautery set at forty-forty to drop the bladder and dissect lymph nodes. The obturator nerve is exposed, and the surgeon clips and seals lymphatics around the vein, ensuring no lymphatics are left behind.

The surgeon performs bladder neck dissection, using a low cautery setting to minimize charring. An incision is made over the prostate, with the left hand applying backward pressure to separate the prostate from the bladder. The urethra is lifted with the fourth arm to identify the posterior bladder neck. The incision starts at the corners, releasing them to maintain a tight bladder neck, and proceeds to the six o'clock position. The bladder neck fibers are visible, and the surgeon removes bladder muscle fibers from the lateral aspects of the prostate base, exposing fat tissue. The bladder is positioned low, away from the surgical field, as the rectal dissection begins.

The surgeon makes an incision in the posterior Denonvilliers' fascia, entering the perirectal fat plane and peeling it off the backside of the prostate. The high anterior release of the prostatic fascia is performed to identify the neurovascular bundle. The robotic clip applier is used to take the pedicle close to the prostate, avoiding injury to the neurovascular bundle visible at the six o'clock position on the screen. The surgeon carefully navigates around the prostate to maintain nerve sparing, cutting the Denonvilliers' edge medially to free the neurovascular bundle.

The surgeon cuts through the dorsal venous complex (DVC) and oversews it, minimizing bleeding due to the low pneumo pressure. The urethra is exposed, and the catheter is reinserted before cutting the urethra to maximize its length. The prostatectomy specimen shows effective nerve sparing, with the pedicle taken close to the prostate. A figure-of-eight stitch is used to secure the DVC, compressing it against the pubic symphysis. A Rocco stitch is placed horizontally under the urethra, incorporating the cut edge of the non-vascular structures and posterior bladder to prevent postoperative bleeding.

The surgeon uses a Stratafix barbed suture to perform the anastomosis, starting at the four or five o'clock position. Two stitches are placed through the urethra and three through the bladder, cinching them down to complete the posterior aspect of the anastomosis. The mucosa is carefully aligned to prevent leaks, ensuring no gaps are left for urine escape. After completing the anastomosis, the catheter is inserted, and the bladder is filled with saline to check for leaks.
